hints.Iesus: 'Iesus' is a proper noun — the subject of the sentence
hints.sapientia: 'sapientia' is a noun meaning 'wisdom' in the ablative

hints.to profit: English 'profit' derives from this root, but Latin 'proficere' means 'to advance/progress'
hints.to throw forward: 'to throw forward' is 'proicere' — 'proficere' means 'to make progress' (pro + facere)

hints.Present: Present would be 'proficit' — 'proficiebat' with -ieba- is imperfect

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the parts of 'facere' (to make/do) — 'proficere' is its compound
hints.proicio, proicere, proieci, proiectum: These are the parts of 'proicere' (to throw forward)
hints.proficiscor, proficisci, profectus sum: These are the parts of 'proficisci' (to set out) — a deponent verb, different from 'proficere'

hints.Et: 'Et' is a conjunction — it cannot be the subject
hints.sapientia: 'sapientia' is in the ablative — it expresses the respect in which Jesus advanced
hints.proficiebat: 'proficiebat' is the verb itself — not the subject

hints.Nominative: 'sapientia' could be nominative, but here it is ablative of respect (advanced in wisdom)
hints.Vocative: Vocative would be used for direct address — 'sapientia' is ablative of respect here
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'sapientiam' — 'sapientia' is the ablative form

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae

translation: And Jesus was advancing in wisdom
